rulings.text: Marbury v. Madison, 5 U.S. (1 Cranch) 137 (1803): the judicial power includes the authority to declare acts of Congress repugnant to the Constitution void.
Gideon v. Wainwright, 372 U.S. 335 (1963) held that indigent defendants are entitled to appointed counsel in all criminal prosecutions, including petty offenses.
rulings.response:
Gideon v. Wainwright, 372 U.S. 335 (1963), held that the Sixth Amendment right to appointed counsel is fundamental and applies to the states through the Fourteenth Amendment; the case arose from a felony prosecution. Its extension to misdemeanors came later and only where actual imprisonment is imposed, Argersinger v. Hamlin, 407 U.S. 25 (1972); it does not reach every petty offense.

Rulings address only (i) whether a cited authority exists and (ii) whether a stated characterization fairly reads the cited text; they are not advice about any person's rights or situation and may not be relied on as such.
